sentences.text: Oh, it's-- you can't explain.
I was a wild girl.
I was enjoying life.
I was a [NON-ENGLISH].
I was carrying on like a lunatic.
Because in school, it was very-- I was a good student.
Let's put this way.
But really and truly, it's a different life in Europe than in America.
We used to dance in the street, jumping, [NON-ENGLISH], walking, and-- and enjoying life.
We didn't know what-- I mean, killing?
Or, are you a Jew?
Are you not a Jew, or what?
We know when you work, you have to pay your taxes.
So who knew when I was 17, 18, 20, who knew-- I was married when I was 20.
Who knew what goes on?
Life goes on wonderful.
And we had stores, we had nice clothes.
We were living a nice life.
I-- I love America very, very much.
From the day we came to America, I always explain to the people, you should hug and kiss the rocks on the streets and the stones on the streets.
But we had a different social life in Poland.
We was a-- my mother was walking out.
And she didn't see the neighbor, she ran into the neighbor.
What happened?
Maybe she's sick.
Or, we was close.
It was like one family.
I didn't know there's 5 or 10 or 15 people, these are friends.
The whole world is your friend.
And I was a [NON-ENGLISH].
I was gone with the cans collecting money and-- and talking and dancing.
And I don't know how to explain how life was just luxury.
We was poor.
My family, my home-- I mean, my father and mother, we hardly have enough to eat and dress.
But we was happy.
We was very happy.
And when you think about it, you dream about it, you can't believe it.
You age so fast and never see again the same life.
[SOBBING] But I'm very happy [INAUDIBLE] again.
Thank God one child gave me a nice family and makes a nice living.
And I have no complaints for America.
I love it.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Oh, my first date.
That's funny.
I was a rabbi [INAUDIBLE] daughter.
He wants to kiss me.
And I said, no, you can't kiss me, because I'm rabbi's daughter.
My father will kill me.
He said, your father's not here.
That was my husband.
I said, but I'm not going to lie to him.
So that was my first memory.
And we always used to talk, me and my husband, how I was so foolish.
I was so naive.
I didn't let him give me a kiss.
Really, that time, you didn't trust nobody.
Everybody was so selfish with life.
When we was hiding and my daughter was to my father-in-law the first grandchild.
And his son was the first one married.
So when they started to bomb, and we was running in the bushes.
And my daughter was crying.
Oh, she's scared.
So my father-in-law said-- and I never could forgive him-- let her go.
We want to live.
Let her go.
So they'll kill her.
So what?
You'll have other children.
And that kills me to the day I die.
And that's my daughter.
I didn't let her go.
And it's very-- he didn't mean it.
He didn't mean it.
But we were so scared, everybody.
So you're asking me the question if we told anybody or ask anybody.
Everybody was selfish.
Just, I want to survive.
I don't care if you survive or not.
So for some reason, I guess God lead me to survive.
And after I survived, I did a [SOBBING] lot for all the survivors for all the people.
Which people, when they see me, they recognize me.
I went to the 50th celebration of the survivors.
They-- they was thinking somebody died, a lot of people who wouldn't recognize me.
And they carried on and they screamed.
Everybody was running.
It was so funny.
I have somewhere the picture, you know, from that-- what I did for them.
So maybe that's why I'm-- I'm not a wild woman.
This year, I went through two operations very bad ones.
But I guess God is leaving me for some reason to do some good for-- for the people.
We were talking about when the men were hiding in the barn.
The barn was where, inside the ghetto as well?
No, no-- it was in the ghetto.
It just was when they came in, the German.
It was like three, four months till they made the ghetto.
First, they [INAUDIBLE].
And-- and this was-- they took the Jewish people to work for them.
So for one day, they announced all the gold, what you have and everything you have from gold, jewelry, everything, bring it.
They-- they said all good clothes, they come and pick it up.
Then they said, if they find in a house a half a pound of flour, they're going to kill the whole family.
Bring everything that you have in the house.
And when they [INAUDIBLE] the people, some people hide it.
Some people had, you know, Polish people what they knew them.
They gave it to them and they hide.
And then they made the ghetto.
Of course.
Everything.
No, nothing.
I don't have even one picture from before the war.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, I have pictures with my sister, got it from some people.
Yes.
Well, the barn was in the back where we had a big yard, all the way in the back.
And-- and my house-- we had a couple houses.
My house was right in the front.
So I used to cook.
And the German was not on your house day and night.
You know, when-- they're passing by.
But they didn't come and watch your house.
It was my father-in-law, my brothers, my husband, my brother-in-laws.
Some of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess not long.
Because about three or four months, they-- we was free in the houses.
And then they took out all the Jews to the graves, and they killed them.
They killed a number-- I cannot recall it's 80,000 or 90,000 Jews.
And they was making the-- the graves for months and months.
And they all say they knew that.
But they said they're doing that, like, to hiding.
They-- we didn't know they were going to kill the Jews there and put them there--
--like animals, like animals.
Well, they announced they doing for hiding if the bombs will be bombs.
And nobody could go outside the city and see what's doing there.
I guess the Ukrainian, the Polish.
Because the Jews was in ghetto.
In the-- where I was that time, I had the dysentery.
I had the bloody dysentery.
So my father-in-law said they announced the sick people in the hospitals shouldn't move.
They're going to come and take them.
They moving us to a small city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They're moving everybody to a small city.
So we wasn't scared.
But, more or less, people was thinking that the sick in the hospital, they're going to kill them.
Because we know they killed already so many people.
So my father-in-law got his neighbor a wagon and horses and took me and my daughter in that and sent me to that small town.
My daughter, my sister, they live in Rovne.
She lived at site [INAUDIBLE] 12 kilometer.
That was [INAUDIBLE] I think.
So I was there.
And then when they took out everybody and they killed everybody, then they made the ghetto.
So I went back to the ghetto.
And then I was-- I got well.
And I got the job from the committee, I don't know-- office or what they call them.
And I was working.
What committee was that?
The Jewish offices what would used to give out cards for bread, card for meat, cards for sugar, cards for this, cards-- we was in ration, so much and so much for a person.
Of course.
Everybody knew everybody.
Huh?
What's my opinion?
They came, they stayed in the line.
They cried.
They were afraid, because the German was taking care in the lines.
They shouldn't beat them up.
Or, they shouldn't pull them up and rape them.
And we didn't have our own life.
We didn't-- we was like animals.
We knew what's there to eat if it was enough and to sleep.
And everywhere was like 8, 9, 10 people in a-- in a two rooms.
It wasn't like everybody had an apartment of our own.
A ghetto was a ghetto.
Everybody was in the-- in their rooms.
There was 12 people in the room.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My friends, my cousins, my girlfriend-- I can't remember them in--
We slept all over.
We had good place on the floor, on the chairs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a couple beds.
We didn't have in Rovne running water.
We had-- what they call them-- pumps.
You took a bucket water and you brought.
So you warmed up some water.
You wash yourself-- just washed.
Like, here, they say a-- a sponge bath.
That was too much.
Because we needed water for drinking.
We didn't have running water.
We didn't have bathrooms like we have here.
Outside.
Yes.
In the winter, the houses what we had-- we had gas.
And they warmed up with gas.
Of course, in Europe, a lot of snow, a lot of things.
So every day, we send another person to stay in line to get the food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we had a lot of potatoes-- a lot.
I mean, potatoes and bread.
What you think we had?
Potatoes, bread, and they make the soup.
Everybody took a big spoon and took the bread-- a sweet life.
Believe me, it was terrible.
So you were there for a year in the ghetto?
Yeah.
I think I was for a year, a little more.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we ran away from the ghetto when we heard it's going to be liquidated, the ghetto.
So I and my sister and the two children, we ran way.
And we was hiding.
Well, we were hiding.
First, we was free, because I had a [INAUDIBLE].
And I-- I asked there while my sister was leaving.
They came, and they liquidated my [INAUDIBLE].
They took everything.
So he promised if anything will happen-- they knew already they kill the Jews-- we can come and be with them.
So, of course, we wasn't sleeping in the house.
We were sleeping in the barn.
And we was free.
They gave us food.
But that wasn't for a long time.
It was for the winter.
And mine sister had, like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one room-- and I, my sister, and the two children.
So once, they came to there.
And they wanted to kill us.
That was Ukraine, not the German.
So from then on, we was wandering from place to place hiding.
And then they start to look in the countries for killing Jews.
So the fellow took us on the-- on the barn-- on top of the barn.
What do they call that?
And we were there, laying.
We couldn't even stand up-- laying and sitting up.
And then they started to dig all over.
And his son was in the [INAUDIBLE].
So he came, and he said, they're going to come to look in their place.
So they came up, and they told us.
So we went in the fields.
So we was laying for three days without food, without water, without nothing in the fields.
And then they came.
And they said, it's OK.
They came.
And they looked, and they left.
So what they did, they digged in the barn.
But you can crawl on your knees.
And then farther away, they digged like a grave.
And they put straw and hay.
And, I don't know, they put a blanket or something.
And they make the hole be near a tree.
We should have the lights.
And we were there until we got free.
Was a family that my sister was very close with them.
[INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].
Sonya. ?]
[INAUDIBLE], the same name like my daughter's.
It's named after my mother.
Her child was four years old.
Three years old.
You know, they were so scared, they-- they was quiet.
And the fellow, what he told us to go away.
For the first two days, he didn't know where we are.
So he was walking and the-- that was in the summertime.
And the-- what they call the-- no, the-- the weeds was high.
So we were laying there.
And it was just starting to grow.
So what we was doing, chewing the grass.
And the grass was keeping us moisturized.
Inside barn.
He was walking on their knees crawling up.
And then was in the woods.
He dug a long, long tunnel.
And then he make me the grave there.
So it was enough for us four to lay, lay and sit.
And the light was coming like this, like a tube.
That's what we had.
And he gave us a path.
And that's where we used to do our duty.
And he brought you food?
How did you [INAUDIBLE]?
Yes, yes.
He was giving us food.
Soup and bread.
He used to give us a bottle of water before for the day.
Huh?
Once a day.
Yeah.
The most in the morning.
No, for all the time.
That was about 14 months.
What we do?
There was full-- excuse me-- with lice and [INAUDIBLE].
We had this shirt what we ran away.
That was the shirt that we got free.
She used to come and take our clothes.
And they have oven where they bake and put the clothes in the oven to kill the animals.
And we was full.
I don't know if you know what it means, [INAUDIBLE] on the head.
Once you get the-- you should know how.
I don't know how to call them-- I mean, between the fingers and on the head.
And the hair was like-- sores, sores from the-- excuse me-- from the lice.
What we did?
Nothing.
She used to bring us gas.
But you light gas.
And we used to put on our heads to kill them.
And they was all over.
It's hard to talk to about it.
Nothing.
Nothing, they was-- well, we were talking to them.
My little one I never wanted to mention.
She was a little bit off.
She started crying, scream.
And she was a little off her mind.
So [INAUDIBLE] took her in the house for three days or four days and kept her-- you know, the oven had like a deck and kept her there hiding.
They knew already.
They understood.
And tried to give her some food.
And she washed her and cleaned her.
And she said, she'll be OK.
And she saved her.
But she used to scream.
But the screaming from the grave, they couldn't hear.
Because it was so deep and everything.
But it took a couple months till she-- she used to bring for her special a little bit milk.
And in Europe, when they have flour, they sieve the flour.
And the rest what they sieve, they feed the cattles.
And she used to take this and bake for us bread and make noodles.
I'll never forget when she brought us noodle [INAUDIBLE] for her.
She brought noodles with a little bit milk.
So she said, mom, if we'll come out, if we'll be free, I promise you I'll never-- that time, there was already [INAUDIBLE] 4 and 1/2 years old-- I'll never eat nothing else-- only noodles and milk.
Until today, I said-- until today, she likes noodles and milk.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But one day, she got up in the morning.
And she said to me.
She was talking Yiddish and Russian.
And she said to me, mother, I had a dream.
[INAUDIBLE] What's listen to a child 4 and 1/2 years old?
I said, what kind of dream did you have?
She said, I was somewhere far away in a beautiful home.
And came a fellow, a very, very rich man.
And he said, I'll marry you.
And I'll give you everything your heart desire.
And I'll take you out of here.
Do you think one thing I'll be married, and I'll be rich?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the dream came true.
So I'm very happy for her.
And I live for my children.
My whole life now is involved for my children.
And what position were you when you were inside the grave?
Sick, very sick.
My sister was very sick.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She had women troubles.
And we were sick.
We didn't know if we're alive or we're dead or we're sick.
So you-- I guess you live, I don't know, without medicines, without anything.
And when we walked, our legs were swollen like balloons.
We didn't have clothes.
We didn't have-- I don't know.
I really-- if you ask me, I can't explain.
I don't know how we-- how we-- what we wore and what we did.
I really can't tell, because it's-- you can't explain.
We ran away with heavy coats.
That was our blankets.
We covered ourselves full with clothes, full with everything.
But that was hard.
But I guess-- I don't know, you survive.
You have so much strength to live.
It's not even funny.
Oh, was summer and winter, of course.
Well, in the winter was very cold.
But in the summer was very good, because it's a grave.
And it's cool.
But--
Days or nights, we didn't know [INAUDIBLE] this day.
And we didn't know when it's day and night.
But we, more or less, we saw a little light.
Complain?
The children?
Yeah, they took straw, and they was doing from straw some-- what did they know?
I know only one thing.
My-- when before we was on the barn on top, so through the straw, they saw the chicken running around and the children play.
So they used to say the two girls, why are the chicken running around free?
Why can't we go?
Why can't we go?
Why?
Why?
Please, let us go down.
So what?
So they'll hurt us.
We're not afraid to be hurt.
Let us go, mommy.
Let me go.
My little one was too smart.
Every day, she said, mother, let me go for a half an hour, a little bit.
I just want to run back and forth, and I come back.
It was hard.
It was terrible hard to keep them quiet and to keep them happy.
But I guess when you're scared, you accept everything.
The time was not long.
The days wasn't too long.
You-- you was only thinking, I'll survive.
I'll survive.
And every day came the guy and told us they just took [INAUDIBLE].
They find so many Jews.
And they took them to Rovne, and they kill them.
They find so many Jews.
They took them to Rovne.
That's the good news what he used to bring us.
Finally, one day, he came.
And he said, the war is finished.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when he said that, [SOBBING] I'll never forget it.
You know?
I can't forget that.
[SOBBING]
We left the children with them-- I, my sister.
And they gave us rags.
And [? we ?] [? coat ?] was in the wintertime.
And we put the rags around our feet.
They were swollen.
We-- and we couldn't walk.
It took us a couple days.
And then I took my sister.
And we walked the 12 kilometers to Rovne. with [INAUDIBLE].
And we left the children.
Then a couple days later, he took the children in a wagon and covered them with straw until I find a place where to be.
And he brought the children, that same guy.
He-- they died.
I was sending them packages.
I still send the package.
Last year we supposed to go to see the daughter.
But we got a letter, she died.
And we didn't go.
I, and my daughter, and my granddaughter, they [INAUDIBLE].
We had already the papers and everything to go.
And she died.
So I stopped sending packages.
[SOBBING] So the first bite of bread, the first dollar I made in America, I send it.
Because things [INAUDIBLE] real life.
I'm not so happy my life, but my daughter's alive.
